metadata.title: Meet Gru & Scope Your App: The /v0 Gate
metadata.topic: INFO 7375 WEEK 1 — MEET GRU & SCOPE YOUR APP (E1/4)
metadata.playlist: The Boondoggle Report — Computational Skepticism for AI
metadata.source: INFO 7375 (Computational Skepticism for AI) — Week 1: The Boondoggle Report (25 pts)

beats.narration_text: Ciao — this is Liam. Episode 1 of 4. Before you can evaluate an AI system, you have to specify one precisely. Gru is the fastest way to build that muscle — and its first gate will stop a vague idea cold. Good. This week runs in four episodes: scope your app in Gru, build the full SDD, generate and read the Boondoggle Score, then write the reflection and ship. Every episode has a concrete deliverable. This one has one job: your app passes the /v0 gate. Let's go.

props.topic: INFO 7375 WEEK 1 — MEET GRU & SCOPE YOUR APP (E1/4)
props.segment: Meet Gru & Scope Your App: The /v0 Gate
props.command: Open Gru. Run /help to confirm the system prompt loaded. Then scope your app with /v0.
props.runningText: a phase-gate engine, not a chatbot — pushback from /v0 is the assignment working…
output: Gru confirmed active (system prompt loaded, /help passes)
App named: domain · user · decision · output in one sentence

beats.narration_text: Gru is a software-design-documentation system built on the Irreducibly Human curriculum. You load it as a Claude Project with the Gru system prompt, or you paste the prompt directly into a new session and run /help to confirm it loaded. When /help returns the list of phase commands — /v0 through /s1 — Gru is active. It is not a chatbot. It is a phase-gate engine: it will not move forward until each gate passes. That distinction matters. A chatbot will accept a vague idea and generate something that looks like output. Gru will refuse and tell you exactly what is missing. The gates are: /v0 names the thing being built. /v1 through /v4 build the problem summary, principles, flows, and need statements. /s1 assembles the full Software Design Document. Each gate requires the previous one to pass. You cannot jump to /s1 from /v0. The order is the curriculum.

props.artifactHeading: A phase-gate engine, not a chatbot
artifactLines: Load as a Claude Project with the Gru system prompt — or paste + run /help.
/help returning the phase-command list confirms Gru is active.
Gates in order: /v0 → /v1 → /v2 → /v3 → /v4 → /s1.
Each gate must pass before the next opens — no skipping.
A chatbot generates output from vague input. Gru refuses and names what's missing.
The refusal is the curriculum.
props.sparkLine: Phase-gate engine. /help confirms it's loaded. Order is the lesson.

beats.act: scope a real app — candidates and anti-patterns
beats.narration_text: Before you run /v0, you need an app worth scoping. Good candidates share three properties: a specific domain, a specific user making a specific decision, and an output that is a document, score, flag, or structured artifact — not a conversation. Examples: a finance claim flagger that checks submitted invoices for policy violations, an annotation tool that routes ambiguous medical images to the right reviewer, a clinic scheduler that assigns appointment slots based on patient triage priority, a moderation dashboard that surfaces borderline posts for human review, a test generator that produces domain-specific assessment questions from a syllabus, a calibration tracker that monitors how a team's confidence scores compare to their outcomes. Anti-patterns: 'an AI assistant for X' — too broad, no insertion point, no output you can test. Two or three generic components — too simple, Gru will flag the missing domain knowledge immediately. And thin domain knowledge — if you cannot describe the domain's edge cases, the SDD will expose that. The test before you run /v0: can you name the domain, the user, the decision, and the output in one sentence? If you need two sentences, the scope is still loose.

artifactLines: Good: finance flagger · annotation router · clinic scheduler · moderation dashboard · test generator · calibration tracker.
Required properties: specific domain + specific user + specific decision + artifact output.
Anti-pattern: 'an AI assistant for X' — no insertion point, no testable output.
Anti-pattern: 2–3 generic components — too simple, no domain knowledge exposed.
Anti-pattern: thin domain knowledge — the SDD will find it.
One-sentence test: domain · user · decision · output. Two sentences = scope still loose.
props.sparkLine: One sentence: domain + user + decision + output. If you need two, keep scoping.

beats.narration_text: Run /v0. The gate asks you to name the thing being built — not the problem it solves, not the user it helps, but the thing itself. The exact naming convention: a noun phrase that describes the artifact or system, followed by its insertion point, followed by its output. 'A claim-flagging tool that sits between the invoice intake form and the payment queue and produces a binary pass or flag with a violation reason' passes. 'An AI that helps finance teams with compliance' does not pass — it names a problem, not a thing. If the gate stops you, that is the assignment working. Do not argue with Gru's pushback. Rewrite the one-sentence naming until it is specific enough that the gate passes. Every rewrite is progress — you are learning to specify, not to negotiate. The students who get frustrated by /v0 pushback are the same students who will miss the score's hardest handoffs in Episode 3. The gate is training your eye.

props.artifactHeading: Name the thing, not the problem
artifactLines: /v0 names the artifact: noun phrase + insertion point + output.
Passes: 'A claim-flagging tool that sits between intake and payment queue, producing a binary flag + reason.'
Fails: 'An AI that helps finance teams with compliance.' — names a problem, not a thing.
If the gate stops you: don't argue. Rewrite the one-sentence naming.
Every rewrite = progress in specification, not negotiation.
Pushback from /v0 is evidence the gate is working. That's the goal.
props.sparkLine: Name the artifact, not the problem. Pushback means rewrite, not argue.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Meet Gru and Scope Your App. Gru is a phase-gate engine — load it as a Claude Project or paste the system prompt and confirm /help. /v0 names the thing being built, not the problem it solves: noun phrase, insertion point, output. Pushback is evidence the gate is working — rewrite, don't argue. Good candidates: finance flagger, annotation router, clinic scheduler, moderation dashboard, test generator, calibration tracker. Anti-patterns: 'an AI assistant for X,' too few components, thin domain knowledge. One deliverable: screenshot of the passed /v0. That screenshot is your Week 1 starting artifact.

props.artifactTitle: Meet Gru & Scope Your App: The /v0 Gate
artifactLines: Gru = phase-gate engine. Load via Claude Project or paste + /help to confirm.
/v0 names the artifact: noun phrase + insertion point + output. Not the problem.
Pushback from /v0 = working as designed. Rewrite the naming, don't negotiate.
Anti-patterns: 'AI assistant for X' · too few components · thin domain knowledge.
Deliverable: screenshot of passed /v0 — your Week 1 starting artifact.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this prompt into Gru — with your own app filled in. The prompt reads: I want to build a [app name]. The domain is [domain]. The user is [role]. The system takes in [input] and produces [output — not a decision, an artifact: a flag, a score, a document, a queue entry]. Run /v0. If you get pushback, don't argue — rewrite the one-sentence naming until the gate passes. End quote. That's the full prompt. Fill in the four brackets before you send it. If you're stuck on the output field, remember: the output is never 'a recommendation' or 'a result' — it is the specific artifact the system writes to the world: a PDF, a binary flag with a reason field, a ranked list with confidence scores. After the gate passes, take a screenshot of the passed /v0 exchange — that's your Week 1 starting artifact and the foundation for every episode that follows.
